figures.caption: Figure 1. Geometry-conditioned latent surrogate architecture. (a) The FNO encoder (four spectral-convolution blocks followed by global average pooling) maps a density snapshot to a time-independent latent z ∈ R48. The time-conditioned SIREN decoder reconstructs κ̂(x, y; t) from (z, t, x, y) via FiLM conditioning with multi-scale spatial Fourier features (σs ∈ {1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100}) and sinusoidal time features. The geometry head is a 1D implicit neural representation (INR) decoding (yupper(x), ylower(x)) from the same µ. (b) The Stage-2 U-Net recovers velocity (u, v) and volume fraction f from the reconstructed density, trained independently.

Figure 2. Full-pipeline temporal reconstruction on a held-out simulation (sim 474). Each row shows a flow channel (density κ, volume fraction f , horizontal velocity u, vertical velocity v); columns show ground truth (GT) and model prediction (Pred) at four times. The latent z is obtained by encoding a single late-time snapshot (tmax = 3.9) and is held fixed for the entire trajectory. Density is reconstructed by the Stage-1 decoder from this time-independent latent, while volume fraction and velocities are predicted by the Stage-2 U-Net from the reconstructed density κ̂. Predicted nozzle walls (white/gray), decoded from the same latent, are overlaid on every panel, confirming geometric consistency and showing reconstruction of both later and earlier times from a late-time encoding.
Figure 3. Per-timestep reconstruction error across the 81 test simulations. Absolute per-channel MSE; shaded bands show ± one standard deviation across test simulations.

Figure 4. Effect of early-time encoding on full-pipeline reconstruction (sim 474). Same setup as Fig. 2, but the latent z is obtained from an early-time snapshot (tmin = 0.1) and held fixed for the full trajectory reconstruction. All channels (density κ, volume fraction f , velocities u, v) and geometry are decoded as in the main figure, showing qualitatively similar reconstruction behavior.

Figure 5. Representative failure case on a held-out test simulation (sim 366). This example ranks among the worst 5% of held-out simulations by composite reconstruction error at t = 3.9. The model smooths thin ligaments and under-resolves sharply localized late-time breakup structures, with visible errors also appearing in the macroscopic jet trajectory.
